purpose: Models the self-limiting mechanism that terminates commodity supercycles through the inflation-rates-demand destruction feedback loop, with multiple complementary transmission channels
components: Price-to-inflation channel: commodity prices embed in headline inflation in 3-6 months
Inflation-to-rates channel: central bank tightening with novel dilemma for transition metals
Rates-to-demand channel: demand destruction operates with 12-18 month lag (~2x slower than inflation embedding)
Balance sheet channel: Kaleckian profit redistribution forces consuming-sector borrowing until credit impulse turns negative
CLO amplifier: higher rates compress CLO arbitrage, reducing issuance and tightening loan supply for commodity borrowers
Term premium channel: 5-10bp expansion per 10% copper move, independent of expected policy path
Treasury supply amplification: higher inflation raises debt service, forcing more issuance, widening term premium
current_assessment: All 8 agents confirmed across all 4 debate pairs. 4/4 historical supercycle terminations involved this mechanism. Timing asymmetry (inflation 2x faster than demand destruction) creates volatile transition periods. Open question: whether fiscal policy (IRA, EU Green Deal) can partially override demand destruction channel.
limitations: Timing asymmetry creates volatile transition period that is difficult to trade
Fiscal subsidies for transition metals may weaken rate-to-demand-destruction channel — never tested at this scale
Interaction between multiple channels is not quantified
Government-financed vs. private-financed transition produces fundamentally different fragility profiles
